The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}} * [[伊勢市]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

revision.text:
{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|12=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|12=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|12=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44th year 5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48th year 3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013rd year 8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}} * [[伊勢市]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

[[ja:土宮]] [[sn:Tsuchi-no-Miya]] [[en:Tsuchi-no-Miya]] [[sn:suchi-no-Miya]]

{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|12=draft|12=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|12=draft|12=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[伊勢市]]

{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|12=draft|12=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|12=draft|12=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44th year 5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48th year 3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013rd year 8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Ise, Mie]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44th year 5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48th year 3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013rd year 8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Ise, Mie]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

revision.comment: 16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:土宮 up to rev 98026781
revision.text:
{{神社 |画像=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格=[[豊受大神宮]]別宮 |創建=[[997年]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''土宮'''（つちのみや）は[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]]にある[[伊勢神宮]][[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の境内別宮である。第62回[[神宮式年遷宮]]では、[[2015年]]（平成27年）[[1月28日]]に[[遷御]]の儀が執行された<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher=[[伊勢志摩経済新聞]]|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 土宮は外宮正宮南方の檜尾山（ひのきおやま）の麓にある外宮の別宮である。別宮とは「'''わけみや'''」の意味で、正宮に次ぎ尊いとされる<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|和書|publisher=伊勢市観光協会|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=伊勢市観光協会/別宮|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
外宮の別宮は土宮のほか、境内に[[多賀宮]]（たかのみや）と[[風宮]]（かぜのみや）、境外に[[月夜見宮]]（つきよみのみや）がある。土宮は多賀宮に次ぎ古く、多賀宮・月夜見宮に次ぎ3位である。外宮境内にある別宮は、多賀宮・'''土宮'''・風宮の順に参拝するのが古来の習わしとされる<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>。
正宮前の池の横の'''亀石'''を渡った先の石段の左に風宮が、右に土宮がある<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref>。亀石は[[高倉山 (伊勢市)|高倉山の天岩戸]]の入り口の岩を運んだと伝えられている。土宮の左手には外宮所管社の[[下御井神社]]がある。
== 祭神 == 土宮の祭神は、外宮所在地・山田の原の[[地主]]の神である'''大土乃御祖神'''（おおつちのみおやのかみ）とされる<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>。
[[度会行忠]]が[[1285年]]（[[弘安]]8年）に記した『神名秘書』では、山田原地主の[[年神|大歳神]]、[[宇迦魂神]]、土御祖神の土宮3座と記載する<ref name="as23"/>。また[[神体]]は大歳神と土御祖神が鏡、宇迦魂神が宝壺であったとする<ref name="as23"/>。
== 歴史 == 記録上の土宮の初出は[[997年]]（[[長徳]]3年）の『長徳検録』とされる。[[927年]]（[[延長 (日本)|延長]]5年）の『[[延喜式神名帳]]』には記載されていない。『長徳検録』とする根拠は、[[度会家行]]の『類聚神祇本源』での引用による。
『類聚神祇本源』によれば、『長徳検録』に外宮所管の田社33前の1座の'''土御祖社'''と記された<ref name="as23"/>。「田社」は現在の末社に相当するものとされ、現在の摂社以下の扱いであった<ref name="as23"/>。
[[大治 (日本)|大治]]3年[[6月3日 (旧暦)|6月3日]]（[[ユリウス暦]]：[[1128年]][[7月2日]]）、土御祖社は[[宮川 (三重県)|宮川]]の外宮禰宜の申請が朝廷に認められ、別宮に昇格し土宮となった<ref name="as23"/>。そのころ度重なる宮川の氾濫に悩まされた外宮禰宜が、土御祖神が氾濫を治めたとして宮号を申請したものであった<ref name="as23"/>。
[[文永]]年間の遷宮では、土宮の瑠璃壺と鏡2面を大物忌が落したことで解任された<ref name="as23"/>。
== 祭事 == 皇大神宮に準じた祭事が行なわれ、[[祈年祭]]、[[月次祭]]、[[神嘗祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]の諸祭には[[皇室]]からの[[幣帛]]（へいはく）がある。幣帛を受けるようになったのは、別宮昇格以降と考えられる<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref>。皇室の勅使は正宮と多賀宮のみに参行し、土宮には参行しない。
=== 式年遷宮 === 土宮の第62回式年遷宮の諸祭と日程は以下の通りである<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=月讀宮以下12別宮の遷宮 - 伊勢神宮|publisher=神宮司庁|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
'''2014年''' * [[8月10日]] - 立柱祭（りっちゅうさい）・上棟祭（じょうとうさい） * [[9月15日]] - 檐付祭（のきつけさい） * [[10月25日]] - 甍祭（いらかさい） '''2015年''' * [[1月26日]] - 御戸祭（みとさい）・御船代奉納式（みふなしろほうのうしき）・洗清（あらいきよめ） * [[1月27日]] - 杵築祭（こつきさい）・後鎮祭（ごちんさい）・川原大祓（かわらおおはらい） * [[1月28日]] - 御飾（おかざり）・遷御（せんぎょ） * [[1月29日]] - 大御饌（おおみけ）・奉幣（ほうへい）
上記のうち、立柱祭・上棟祭・杵築祭・後鎮祭・遷御・奉幣は、[[今上天皇]]による御治定（ごじじょう、日程の御定め）に基づく<ref name="sg"/>。
== 社殿 == 土宮の社殿は外宮に準じ[[千木・鰹木|外削ぎの千木と、5本で奇数の鰹木]]を持つ萱葺の[[神明造]]である。神宮の境内別宮は基本的に南面しているが、土宮は東面している<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref>。遷宮のための古殿地は通常は東西に並ぶが、土宮は南北に近い。別宮昇格直後に南面させるべきとの声があったが、従来のままとされた<ref name="as24"/>。またの境内別宮は基本的に鳥居を持たないが、土宮は鳥居を持つ。
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[伊勢市駅]] * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]外宮前バス停 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ]] * 駐車場：無料駐車場がある。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44年5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48年3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013年8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会
== 関連項目 == * [[豊受大神宮]] * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[伊勢市]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}} {{神宮125社}} {{デフォルトソート:つちのみや}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:2015年竣工の日本の建築物]]

{{神社 |画像=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格=[[豊受大神宮]]別宮 |創建=[[997年]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''土宮'''（つちのみや）は[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]]にある[[伊勢神宮]][[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の境内別宮である。第62回[[神宮式年遷宮]]では、[[2015年]]（平成27年）[[1月28日]]に[[遷御]]の儀が執行された<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher=[[伊勢志摩経済新聞]]|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 土宮は外宮正宮南方の檜尾山（ひのきおやま）の麓にある外宮の別宮である。別宮とは「'''わけみや'''」の意味で、正宮に次ぎ尊いとされる<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|和書|publisher=伊勢市観光協会|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=伊勢市観光協会/別宮|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
外宮の別宮は土宮のほか、境内に[[多賀宮]]（たかのみや）と[[風宮]]（かぜのみや）、境外に[[月夜見宮]]（つきよみのみや）がある。土宮は多賀宮に次ぎ古く、多賀宮・月夜見宮に次ぎ3位である。外宮境内にある別宮は、多賀宮・'''土宮'''・風宮の順に参拝するのが古来の習わしとされる<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>。
正宮前の池の横の'''亀石'''を渡った先の石段の左に風宮が、右に土宮がある<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref>。亀石は[[高倉山 (伊勢市)|高倉山の天岩戸]]の入り口の岩を運んだと伝えられている。土宮の左手には外宮所管社の[[下御井神社]]がある。
== 祭神 == 土宮の祭神は、外宮所在地・山田の原の[[地主]]の神である'''大土乃御祖神'''（おおつちのみおやのかみ）とされる<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>。
[[度会行忠]]が[[1285年]]（[[弘安]]8年）に記した『神名秘書』では、山田原地主の[[年神|大歳神]]、[[宇迦魂神]]、土御祖神の土宮3座と記載する<ref name="as23"/>。また[[神体]]は大歳神と土御祖神が鏡、宇迦魂神が宝壺であったとする<ref name="as23"/>。
== 歴史 == 記録上の土宮の初出は[[997年]]（[[長徳]]3年）の『長徳検録』とされる。[[927年]]（[[延長 (日本)|延長]]5年）の『[[延喜式神名帳]]』には記載されていない。『長徳検録』とする根拠は、[[度会家行]]の『類聚神祇本源』での引用による。
『類聚神祇本源』によれば、『長徳検録』に外宮所管の田社33前の1座の'''土御祖社'''と記された<ref name="as23"/>。「田社」は現在の末社に相当するものとされ、現在の摂社以下の扱いであった<ref name="as23"/>。
[[大治 (日本)|大治]]3年[[6月3日 (旧暦)|6月3日]]（[[ユリウス暦]]：[[1128年]][[7月2日]]）、土御祖社は[[宮川 (三重県)|宮川]]の外宮禰宜の申請が朝廷に認められ、別宮に昇格し土宮となった<ref name="as23"/>。そのころ度重なる宮川の氾濫に悩まされた外宮禰宜が、土御祖神が氾濫を治めたとして宮号を申請したものであった<ref name="as23"/>。
[[文永]]年間の遷宮では、土宮の瑠璃壺と鏡2面を大物忌が落したことで解任された<ref name="as23"/>。
== 祭事 == 皇大神宮に準じた祭事が行なわれ、[[祈年祭]]、[[月次祭]]、[[神嘗祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]の諸祭には[[皇室]]からの[[幣帛]]（へいはく）がある。幣帛を受けるようになったのは、別宮昇格以降と考えられる<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref>。皇室の勅使は正宮と多賀宮のみに参行し、土宮には参行しない。
=== 式年遷宮 === 土宮の第62回式年遷宮の諸祭と日程は以下の通りである<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=月讀宮以下12別宮の遷宮 - 伊勢神宮|publisher=神宮司庁|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>。
'''2014年''' * [[8月10日]] - 立柱祭（りっちゅうさい）・上棟祭（じょうとうさい） * [[9月15日]] - 檐付祭（のきつけさい） * [[10月25日]] - 甍祭（いらかさい） '''2015年''' * [[1月26日]] - 御戸祭（みとさい）・御船代奉納式（みふなしろほうのうしき）・洗清（あらいきよめ） * [[1月27日]] - 杵築祭（こつきさい）・後鎮祭（ごちんさい）・川原大祓（かわらおおはらい） * [[1月28日]] - 御飾（おかざり）・遷御（せんぎょ） * [[1月29日]] - 大御饌（おおみけ）・奉幣（ほうへい）
上記のうち、立柱祭・上棟祭・杵築祭・後鎮祭・遷御・奉幣は、[[今上天皇]]による御治定（ごじじょう、日程の御定め）に基づく<ref name="sg"/>。
== 社殿 == 土宮の社殿は外宮に準じ[[千木・鰹木|外削ぎの千木と、5本で奇数の鰹木]]を持つ萱葺の[[神明造]]である。神宮の境内別宮は基本的に南面しているが、土宮は東面している<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref>。遷宮のための古殿地は通常は東西に並ぶが、土宮は南北に近い。別宮昇格直後に南面させるべきとの声があったが、従来のままとされた<ref name="as24"/>。またの境内別宮は基本的に鳥居を持たないが、土宮は鳥居を持つ。
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[伊勢市駅]] * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]外宮前バス停 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ]] * 駐車場：無料駐車場がある。

{{神道 横}} {{神宮125社}} {{デフォルトソート:つちのみや}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:2015年竣工の日本の建築物]]

{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==

* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsuchi-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:2015th year 竣工の日本の建築物]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:qq]] }}
[[ja:土宮]] [[sn:Tsuchi-no-Miya]] [[en:Tsuchi-no-Miya]] [[sn:suchi-no-Miya]]

{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Ise, Mie]]

{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR|qq=}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangu Line|qq=}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise, Mie|qq=}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}. During the 62nd {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** {{ill|JR|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR|qq=}} {{ill|Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangu Line|qq=}} & {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}} {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} * **Nearest Bus Stop:** {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}} {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
