{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44th year 5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48th year 3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013rd year 8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423156]] Wikidata name: Tsuchi-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |256px|土宮]] |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44th year 5月20日 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48th year 3月25日 * [[三橋健 (神道学者)|三橋健]]『伊勢神宮 日本人は何を祈ってきたのか』[[朝日新書]]416、[[朝日新聞出版]]、2013rd year 8月30日、230p. ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsuchi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/geku_3.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 伊勢神宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0212.html 土宮（つちのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423156]] Wikidata name: Tsuchi-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie |prefecture={{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.485778|N|136.70333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=|1=Sengyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892914}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=|1=ISESHIMA KEIZAI SHIMBUN|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700592}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.

[[ja:土宮]] [[sn:Tsuchi-no-Miya]]
[[sn:suchi-no-Miya]] [[Category:Buildings in Japan completed in 2015]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchi-no-Miya]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tsuchi-no-miya}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423156]] Wikidata name: Tsuchi-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie |prefecture={{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.485778|N|136.70333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=|1=Sengyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892914}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=|1=ISESHIMA KEIZAI SHIMBUN|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700592}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.

Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423156]] Wikidata name: Tsuchi-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie |prefecture={{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.485778|N|136.70333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=|1=Sengyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892914}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=|1=ISESHIMA KEIZAI SHIMBUN|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700592}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.
== Access ==
* **Nearest Station:** [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangu Line]] & [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] * **Nearest Bus Stop:** [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Geku-mae Bus Stop * **Nearest IC:** [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] {{ill|Ise-Nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Nishi IC|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} * **Parking:** Free parking available. == References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-02-273516-4 * 『お伊勢まいり』伊勢神宮崇敬会 == See Also == * {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]

Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.

According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsuchi-no-miya]][[ja:土宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423156]] Wikidata name: Tsuchi-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Tsuchinomiya.jpg |名称=土宮 |所在地=[[三重県]][[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] |祭神=大土乃御祖神 |社格={{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}別宮 |創建=[[997]]以前 |本殿=[[神明造]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 29|緯度秒 = 8.8 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 12 |例祭= |地図=Japan Mie |prefecture={{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.485778|N|136.70333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|qq=|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|qq=|1=Sengyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892914}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|qq=|1=ISESHIMA KEIZAI SHIMBUN|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700592}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.

In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.
=== Shikinen Sengu (Periodic Reconstruction) ===
The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>
== Shrine Architecture ==
The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west. Although there were suggestions to reorient the shrine southward after its elevation, it has remained as originally aligned.<ref name="as24"/>
Unlike other betsu-gu in the precinct, Tsuchi-no-Miya also features a torii gate.

'''Tsuchi-no-Miya''' (土宮) is an auxiliary shrine (betsugū) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū) in {{ill|Toyokawa-chō, Ise|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokawa-chō, Ise|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. During the 62nd {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}, the ritual of {{ill|Sengu|ja|遷御|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengu|1=Sengyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892914}} was conducted on January 28, 2015 (Heisei 27).<ref name="ik1501">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|title=伊勢神宮外宮別宮「土宮」宮川堤防の守護神も新宮にお引っ越し|publisher={{ill|Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|ja|伊勢志摩経済新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-Shima Keizai Shimbun|1=ISESHIMA KEIZAI SHIMBUN|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700592}}|date=2015-01-30|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210060655/http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2240/|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Tsuchi-no-Miya is a betsu-gu (secondary shrine) of the Geku (Outer Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine, located at the foot of Hinokioyama, south of the Geku's main sanctuary. The term "betsu-gu" (別宮) means "separate shrine" and denotes a shrine that holds great reverence, second only to the main sanctuary.<ref name="bg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|url=http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|title=Ise City Tourism Association/Betsu-gu|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210044250/http://www.ise-kanko.jp/jingu/betugu.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
In addition to Tsuchi-no-Miya, the Geku has the following betsu-gu: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}}. Tsuchi-no-Miya is the second oldest after Taka-no-Miya and Ranks third following Taka-no-Miya and Tsukiyomi-no-Miya. The traditional order of visiting the betsu-gu within the Geku precincts is Taka-no-Miya, Tsuchi-no-Miya, and then Kaze-no-Miya.<ref>三橋（2013）：121 - 122ページ</ref>
Kaze-no-Miya is located to the left of the stone steps after crossing the "Kameishi" (Turtle Stone) next to the pond in front of the main sanctuary, and Tsuchi-no-Miya is on the right.<ref>三橋（2013）：122ページ</ref> The Kameishi is said to have been brought from the entrance of the Ama-no-Iwato cave of Mt. Takakura. To the left of Tsuchi-no-Miya is {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}}, a shrine under the jurisdiction of the Geku.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The deity enshrined at Tsuchi-no-Miya is "Otsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami" (大土乃御祖神), the land god of Yamada-no-hara, where the Geku is located.<ref name="as23">三橋（2013）：123ページ</ref>
According to "Jinmei Hisyo" written by Watarai Yukitada in 1285, the shrine originally enshrined three deities: "Otoshi-no-Kami," "Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami," and "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami."<ref name="as23"/> It is said that the sacred objects representing these deities were mirrors for Otoshi-no-Kami and Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami, and a treasure jar for Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami.<ref name="as23"/>
== History ==
The first recorded mention of Tsuchi-no-Miya appears in "Chotoku Kenroku" from 997. It is not listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" compiled in 927. This identification is based on a citation in "Ruiju Jingi Hongen" by Watarai Ieyuki.
According to "Ruiju Jingi Hongen," "Chotoku Kenroku" refers to Tsuchi-no-Miya as "Tsuchi-no-Mioya-sha," one of 33 "Ta-sha" under the Geku's jurisdiction.<ref name="as23"/> "Ta-sha" corresponds to today's minor shrines, suggesting that Tsuchi-no-Miya was initially regarded as a subordinate shrine.<ref name="as23"/>
On June 3, 1128 (Daiji 3), the shrine was elevated to betsu-gu status by imperial decree following a petition from the Geku priests. This elevation was attributed to Tsuchi-no-Mioya-no-Kami's perceived role in calming frequent floods of the Miyagawa River.<ref name="as23"/>
During the Bun'ei era, an incident occurred where the "Omikura" (sacred vessels) and two mirrors were accidentally dropped, leading to the dismissal of the responsible official.<ref name="as23"/>
== Festivals ==
Festivals held at Tsuchi-no-Miya follow the same rites as those at the main sanctuary, including the "Kinen-sai," "Tsukinami-sai," "Kannamesai," and "Niinamesai." Imperial offerings are made during these festivals, a practice that began after the shrine's elevation to betsu-gu status.<ref>三橋（2013）：123 - 124ページ</ref> However, imperial envoys only visit the main sanctuary and Taka-no-Miya, not Tsuchi-no-Miya.

The 62nd Shikinen Sengu ceremonies for Tsuchi-no-Miya took place as follows:<ref name="ik1501"/><ref name="sg">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|title=Reconstruction of Tsukiyomi-no-Miya and 12 other Betsu-gu - Ise Jingu|publisher=Jingu Administration Office|accessdate=2015-02-10|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210061619/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/sikinen_2.html|archivedate=2015-02-10}}</ref>
**2014** * August 10 - Ritchusai (Pillar-erecting Ceremony) & Jotosai (Roof-raising Ceremony) * September 15 - Nokitsukesai (Eaves-attachment Ceremony) * October 25 - Irakasai (Roof Ridge Ceremony)
**2015** * January 26 - Mitosai, Mifunashiro Dedication Ceremony, and Araikiyome (Purification) * January 27 - Kotsukisai, Gochinsai (Foundation Ceremony), and Kawara Oharae (River Purification) * January 28 - Okazari (Decoration) & Sengu (Transfer of the Deity) * January 29 - Omike (Sacred Meal) & Hohei (Offering Ceremony)
The above ceremonies were scheduled based on imperial decree.<ref name="sg"/>

The shrine's architecture follows the "Shinmei-zukuri" style with thatched roofs, "chigi" (forked roof finials), and "katsuogi" (log-like roof decorations), all facing east unlike most betsu-gu, which face south.<ref name="as24">三橋（2013）：124ページ</ref> The old shrine site for reconstruction is aligned north-south rather than the usual east-west.
